summary: Gruber examines the Neil Young–Joe Rogan Spotify controversy, arguing that Young's decision to pull his music was a genuine principled stand, not an attempt to get Rogan removed. He contends that free speech works both ways: Rogan can say what he wants, and Young can refuse association with Spotify. Gruber notes that Rogan's exclusivity deal with Spotify actually reduced his reach, so claims of censorship ring hollow. He draws a critical distinction between Spotify's exclusive hosting model and Apple's open podcast directory, pushing back on those who equate Apple indexing Steve Bannon's podcast with Spotify paying Rogan $100 million. The piece concludes that demanding search engines delist content crosses from principled objection into opposition to free speech itself.
key_points: Neil Young's withdrawal from Spotify was genuine principle — forcing Spotify to publicly own its support for Rogan — not a failed attempt to get Rogan removed
Free speech is bidirectional: Rogan's right to speak doesn't negate Young's right to disassociate and publicize his stance
Rogan's Spotify exclusivity deal actually shrank his audience; breaking the deal would likely restore and grow his reach, undermining censorship claims
There is a fundamental distinction between Spotify exclusively hosting and paying for content versus Apple indexing open podcast feeds like a search engine
Demanding that search engines and open directories delist content crosses from principled objection into opposing free speech
money_quotes: The answer to speech one disagrees with is more speech, and this is more speech.
Cynicism runs so deep right now that many people overlook the obvious: that Young is putting his money where his mouth is by pulling his catalog from Spotify. There is no catch.
Genuine virtue, not mere virtue signaling.
Tell people not to read or listen to someone, and people naturally get curious what it is that person has to say.
If you think the world would be a better place if fewer people listened to Rogan's show, the status quo, where his show is exclusive to Spotify, is the best you're going to get.
Apple, clearly, does not host Steve Bannon's podcast. Apple's podcast directory is akin to a search engine; they index the feeds of open podcasts.
But that, to me, crosses the line into being against free speech.
key_insight: Young's Spotify exit was principled free speech in action — not censorship of Rogan — and the crucial distinction between paying to exclusively host content and indexing open feeds determines where the line between principled objection and suppression of speech actually falls.
